aiMessages.content:
Build a simple WhatsApp customer support test bot.

The bot should be called WhatsApp Test Assistant.

This is a WhatsApp bot that will be connected through CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport. Do not add WhatsApp Cloud API setup, WhatsApp Web session setup, QR code logic, phone number ID setup, webhook verification for Meta, or any direct WhatsApp API code. CookMyBots handles the WhatsApp connection and transport.

The generated bot should act as the independent bot brain.

Core behavior:
- When a user says hello, hi, hey, or good morning, reply with a friendly greeting.
- When a user asks what the bot does, explain that this is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots.
- When a user types pricing, price, or cost, reply with a simple pricing explanation.
- When a user types help, show a short list of available commands.
- When a user types contact, reply with a placeholder support contact message.
- For any other message, reply naturally and say that the bot received the message.

Commands and examples:
- help
- pricing
- contact
- about

Reply style:
- Keep replies short and WhatsApp-friendly.
- Do not use long paragraphs.
- Do not use markdown tables.
- Do not spam.
- Sound friendly and professional.

Technical requirements:
- Expose POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp.
- The webhook must verify the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header using CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET.
- The webhook must accept from, text, messageId, projectId, platform, source, and timestamp.
- The webhook must return JSON with ok: true and reply.
- Add a /health endpoint.
- Add a /test endpoint for local testing.
- Do not call WhatsApp APIs directly.
- Do not require WHATSAPP_TOKEN, WHATSAPP_PHONE_NUMBER_ID, WHATSAPP_VERIFY_TOKEN, SESSION_ID, or QR_SESSION.
- Use only CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

Testing goal:
When I connect a WhatsApp number inside CookMyBots and send “hello”, the bot should reply:
“Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.”

Built the WhatsApp-only CookMyBots bot brain with deterministic replies, secured webhook, health check, local test endpoint, safe logging, and updated docs/env configuration.

Validation: ✅ project passes syntax/basic checks after this edit.

planMarkdown:
Bot: WhatsApp Test Assistant

Summary:
WhatsApp Test Assistant is a simple production-ready customer support test bot for WhatsApp. It runs as an independent Node.js ESM bot brain service and connects to WhatsApp only through CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

The bot does not manage WhatsApp sessions, QR codes, Meta webhook verification, phone number IDs, WhatsApp tokens, or direct WhatsApp API calls. CookMyBots handles all WhatsApp transport concerns. The service only receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots, decides what to reply, and returns the reply in the webhook response.

The first target behavior is intentionally simple: when a WhatsApp user sends “hello”, the bot should respond with: “Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.”

Product goals:
1) Provide a simple WhatsApp support test experience.
The bot should make it easy to confirm that a WhatsApp number connected in CookMyBots can send messages to the bot brain and receive replies.

2) Keep replies short and WhatsApp-friendly.
Responses should be concise, friendly, and professional. The bot should avoid long paragraphs, tables, or excessive follow-up messages.

3) Support a small command set.
The bot should understand greetings, help, pricing, contact, and about-style questions. It should also handle unknown messages gracefully by acknowledging that the message was received.

4) Stay transport-independent.
The bot project is only the brain service. It should not include WhatsApp Cloud API logic, WhatsApp Web session logic, QR session setup, phone number ID setup, or direct WhatsApp API calls.

5) Be production-ready despite simple behavior.
The service should include request validation, webhook secret verification, health checks, local testing support, structured logging, safe error handling, and clear environment configuration.

High-level architecture:
The system is a single Node.js ESM service running as a bot brain behind CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

Runtime components:

1) HTTP server
The service exposes three main endpoints:

1) POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp
This is the production inbound webhook endpoint used by CookMyBots. It receives normalized WhatsApp message events, verifies the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header against CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET, routes the message to the bot logic, and returns JSON containing ok: true and reply.

2) GET /health
This endpoint confirms that the service is alive. It should return a simple health response suitable for uptime checks.

3) POST /test
This endpoint is for local development and QA. It accepts a test message payload similar to the CookMyBots webhook shape and returns the same reply decision without requiring live WhatsApp traffic. It should not send any WhatsApp messages directly.

2) Webhook security layer
The webhook endpoint must reject requests when the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header is missing or does not match CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET. This prevents unauthorized systems from triggering bot replies.

3) Request normalization layer
The webhook accepts these fields from CookMyBots: from, text, messageId, projectId, platform, source, and timestamp.

The bot should normalize text by trimming whitespace and comparing lowercase versions for command detection. The original text may be retained for logging or fallback replies.

4) Intent router
A simple deterministic router is enough for this test bot. It should classify messages into these intent groups:

1) Greeting: hello, hi, hey, good morning
2) Pricing: pricing, price, cost
3) Help: help
4) Contact: contact
5) About: about, what do you do, what does this bot do, who are you
6) Fallback: anything else

5) Reply composer
The reply composer returns short WhatsApp-friendly text. It should produce exactly the expected greeting response for “hello”: Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

6) Optional persistence layer
For this simple bot, persistence is optional. In-memory maps may be used for lightweight request deduplication or basic per-user counters. MongoDB may be used if message logs, audit trails, or user conversation history are needed.

7) Logging and observability
The service should log inbound request metadata such as messageId, from, projectId, platform, source, detected intent, and processing result. Logs should avoid storing sensitive full message content unless explicitly needed for debugging and allowed by policy.

Data model:
The bot can operate without a database. However, the following practical data model is recommended if MongoDB persistence is enabled.

1) MessageEvent
Purpose: stores inbound message metadata for debugging, analytics, and auditability.

Fields:
1) messageId: unique identifier from CookMyBots
2) from: sender identifier provided by CookMyBots
3) projectId: CookMyBots project identifier
4) platform: expected to be whatsapp
5) source: source identifier from CookMyBots
6) text: inbound user message, optionally redacted depending on privacy needs
7) normalizedText: trimmed lowercase version used for routing
8) intent: detected intent such as greeting, pricing, help, contact, about, or fallback
9) reply: response returned by the bot
10) timestamp: original event timestamp from CookMyBots
11) receivedAt: server receive time
12) processedAt: server processing completion time
13) status: processed, duplicate, rejected, or failed

2) UserState
Purpose: optional lightweight state for future personalization or test analytics.

Fields:
1) from: sender identifier
2) firstSeenAt: first message time
3) lastSeenAt: latest message time
4) messageCount: number of processed messages
5) lastIntent: most recent detected intent
6) lastReplyAt: latest reply time

3) IdempotencyRecord
Purpose: prevents duplicate processing if CookMyBots retries delivery of the same message.

Fields:
1) messageId: unique inbound message identifier
2) from: sender identifier
3) reply: reply that was generated for the original delivery
4) createdAt: time the message was first processed

For the initial version, an in-memory map keyed by messageId is sufficient if the service is expected to run as a single process and duplicate protection only needs to last for the current process lifetime. MongoDB is better if duplicate handling should survive restarts.

Key flows:
1) Greeting flow
A user sends “hello”, “hi”, “hey”, or “good morning” in WhatsApp. CookMyBots forwards the normalized message event to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp. The service verifies the webhook secret, detects the greeting intent, and returns: Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

2) Help flow
A user sends “help”. The bot returns a short list of available commands, for example: I can help with: help, pricing, contact, and about.

3) Pricing flow
A user sends “pricing”, “price”, or “cost”. The bot returns a simple pricing explanation, for example: Pricing is not configured yet. This test bot can show placeholder pricing information.

4) Contact flow
A user sends “contact”. The bot returns a placeholder support contact message, for example: You can contact support at support@example.com. Replace this with your real support contact.

5) About flow
A user sends “about” or asks what the bot does. The bot explains that it is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots, for example: I am WhatsApp Test Assistant, a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots.

6) Fallback flow
A user sends any other text. The bot responds naturally and confirms receipt, for example: Thanks, I received your message. Send help to see what I can do.

7) Unauthorized webhook flow
A request reaches POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp without the correct X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header. The service rejects the request and does not run bot logic.

8) Duplicate message flow
If messageId has already been processed, the service should return the same reply if available. This prevents inconsistent behavior during webhook retries.

9) Local test flow
A developer sends a sample request to POST /test with text such as “hello”. The service runs the same intent router and reply composer, then returns the reply. This verifies bot behavior without connecting to WhatsApp directly.

Tech stack:
The bot should use a single Node.js ESM service/process.

Allowed components:

1) Node.js ESM runtime for the bot brain service.
2) An HTTP server framework suitable for exposing POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp, GET /health, and POST /test.
3) Environment variables for configuration, especially CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET.
4) MongoDB only if persistence is needed for message logs, user state, or idempotency records.
5) Simple in-memory maps if persistence is optional or if only lightweight runtime state is needed.
6) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if future file or image handling is added.
7) Generic Node.js server in the cloud for deployment.

Strict transport boundary:

1) WhatsApp connectivity is handled by CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.
2) The bot must not call WhatsApp APIs directly.
3) The bot must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks.
4) The bot must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN, SESSIONID, or QRSESSION.
5) The bot must not include WhatsApp Web session setup, QR code login logic, or Meta webhook verification.

Required environment variables:

1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET: shared secret used to verify the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret request header.
2) NODEENV: runtime environment such as development or production.
3) PORT: HTTP server port.
4) MONGODBURI: optional, only if MongoDB persistence is enabled.

The service should remain simple and dependency-light because the first release is a deterministic command-style test bot.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Security
The production webhook must verify X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret using CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET. Unauthorized requests should be rejected before message processing. Logs should avoid exposing secrets and should avoid unnecessary sensitive message storage.

2) Reliability
The webhook should respond quickly and consistently. For this bot, all logic is local and deterministic, so replies should usually be generated immediately. Duplicate messageIds should be handled safely to support retry behavior.

3) Performance
The bot should be lightweight. String matching and response generation require minimal CPU and memory. A single Node.js process is sufficient for this test assistant.

4) Maintainability
Intent matching should be organized so future commands can be added without changing the transport layer. The webhook handler, validation, intent routing, reply composition, and persistence should remain separate responsibilities.

5) Observability
Each processed webhook should log request metadata, detected intent, response status, and processing duration. The /health endpoint should make basic uptime monitoring straightforward.

6) Privacy
Only store message content if needed. If MongoDB logging is enabled, keep retention limited and avoid storing unnecessary personal data.

7) User experience
Replies should be short, friendly, and professional. The bot should not send multiple messages for a single inbound message. It should not use markdown tables or long paragraphs.

8) Production readiness checklist
1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET configured before deployment.
2) /health returns a successful response.
3) /test confirms “hello” produces the expected greeting.
4) CookMyBots project points to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp.
5) No WhatsApp Cloud API, WhatsApp Web, QR session, or direct WhatsApp token configuration is present.
6) Logs confirm inbound messageId, intent, and successful reply generation.

Bot commands:
• help — Shows a short list of available bot commands. (usage: Send: help) [support]
• pricing — Returns a simple placeholder pricing explanation. The same intent should also match price and cost. (usage: Send: pricing) [sales]
• contact — Returns a placeholder support contact message that can later be replaced with real contact details. (usage: Send: contact) [support]
• about — Explains that this is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots. (usage: Send: about) [information]

DOCS.md.content:
# WhatsApp Test Assistant

WhatsApp Test Assistant is a simple CookMyBots managed WhatsApp bot brain.

CookMyBots handles the WhatsApp connection and transport. This service does not implement WhatsApp Cloud API setup, Meta webhook verification, WhatsApp Web sessions, QR login, phone number ID setup, or direct WhatsApp API calls.

## Endpoints

### GET /health

Returns a simple healthy JSON response for deployment checks.

Example response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "healthy",
  "platform": "whatsapp"
}

### POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport forwards inbound WhatsApp messages to this endpoint.

Required header:

X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret: your configured CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Accepted JSON fields:

1) from
2) text
3) messageId
4) projectId
5) platform
6) source
7) timestamp

Successful response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "reply": "Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do."
}

Invalid or missing webhook secret returns an unauthorized JSON response.

### POST /test

Use this endpoint for local testing without WhatsApp.

Example body:

{
  "text": "hello"
}

Example response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "reply": "Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do."
}

## Supported messages

Send hello, hi, hey, or good morning to receive the welcome reply.

Send help to see available text commands.

Send pricing, price, or cost to receive placeholder pricing information.

Send contact to receive a placeholder support contact message.

Send about, what do you do, what does this bot do, or who are you to learn what the bot does.

Any other message receives a short acknowledgement.

## Public text commands

### help

Shows the available text commands.

Usage: send help

### pricing

Returns placeholder pricing information.

Usage: send pricing, price, or cost

### contact

Returns placeholder support contact information.

Usage: send contact

### about

Explains that this is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots.

Usage: send about

## Environment variables

### CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Required for the production webhook. CookMyBots sends this value in the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header.

### PORT

Optional server port. Defaults to 3000 when missing.

## Local setup

1) Install dependencies with npm install.
2) Copy .env.sample to .env.
3) Set CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET for webhook testing.
4) Start development mode with npm run dev.

## Local test examples

Start the server, then send a local test request to POST http://localhost:3000/test with this JSON body:

{
  "text": "hello"
}

To test the production-style webhook locally, send POST http://localhost:3000/webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp with the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header and a body like:

{
  "from": "local-user",
  "text": "help",
  "messageId": "test-message-1",
  "projectId": "local-project",
  "platform": "whatsapp",
  "source": "cookmybots",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00.000Z"
}

## Deployment notes

Deploy this as one Node.js service. Configure PORT if your host requires it. Configure CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET in the CookMyBots deployment environment.

In CookMyBots, connect your WhatsApp number in the UI. CookMyBots will forward WhatsApp messages to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp and use the JSON reply returned by this service.

## Logging

The service logs startup, environment sanity booleans, webhook receipt, rejected secret verification, successful reply generation, and route failures.

Secrets are never logged.

README.md.content:
# WhatsApp Test Assistant

A WhatsApp-only bot brain for CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

CookMyBots handles WhatsApp connectivity. This repo only receives normalized message events, generates one short reply, and returns it as JSON.

## Endpoints

1) GET /health
Returns deployment health JSON.

2) POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp
Production CookMyBots inbound WhatsApp webhook. Requires X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret.

3) POST /test
Local testing endpoint. Requires only a JSON body with text.

## Environment

Copy .env.sample to .env and configure:

1) CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET
2) PORT

No WhatsApp tokens, phone number IDs, QR sessions, or direct WhatsApp credentials are used.

## Run

1) npm install
2) npm run dev

## Test

POST http://localhost:3000/test

Body:

{
  "text": "hello"
}

Expected reply:

Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

See DOCS.md for full usage details.

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: WhatsApp Test Assistant

Summary:
WhatsApp Test Assistant is a simple production-ready customer support test bot for WhatsApp. It runs as an independent Node.js ESM bot brain service and connects to WhatsApp only through CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

The bot does not manage WhatsApp sessions, QR codes, Meta webhook verification, phone number IDs, WhatsApp tokens, or direct WhatsApp API calls. CookMyBots handles all WhatsApp transport concerns. The service only receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots, decides what to reply, and returns the reply in the webhook response.

The first target behavior is intentionally simple: when a WhatsApp user sends “hello”, the bot should respond with: “Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.”

Product goals:
1) Provide a simple WhatsApp support test experience.
The bot should make it easy to confirm that a WhatsApp number connected in CookMyBots can send messages to the bot brain and receive replies.

2) Keep replies short and WhatsApp-friendly.
Responses should be concise, friendly, and professional. The bot should avoid long paragraphs, tables, or excessive follow-up messages.

3) Support a small command set.
The bot should understand greetings, help, pricing, contact, and about-style questions. It should also handle unknown messages gracefully by acknowledging that the message was received.

4) Stay transport-independent.
The bot project is only the brain service. It should not include WhatsApp Cloud API logic, WhatsApp Web session logic, QR session setup, phone number ID setup, or direct WhatsApp API calls.

5) Be production-ready despite simple behavior.
The service should include request validation, webhook secret verification, health checks, local testing support, structured logging, safe error handling, and clear environment configuration.

High-level architecture:
The system is a single Node.js ESM service running as a bot brain behind CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

Runtime components:

1) HTTP server
The service exposes three main endpoints:

1) POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp
This is the production inbound webhook endpoint used by CookMyBots. It receives normalized WhatsApp message events, verifies the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header against CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET, routes the message to the bot logic, and returns JSON containing ok: true and reply.

2) GET /health
This endpoint confirms that the service is alive. It should return a simple health response suitable for uptime checks.

3) POST /test
This endpoint is for local development and QA. It accepts a test message payload similar to the CookMyBots webhook shape and returns the same reply decision without requiring live WhatsApp traffic. It should not send any WhatsApp messages directly.

2) Webhook security layer
The webhook endpoint must reject requests when the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header is missing or does not match CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET. This prevents unauthorized systems from triggering bot replies.

3) Request normalization layer
The webhook accepts these fields from CookMyBots: from, text, messageId, projectId, platform, source, and timestamp.

The bot should normalize text by trimming whitespace and comparing lowercase versions for command detection. The original text may be retained for logging or fallback replies.

4) Intent router
A simple deterministic router is enough for this test bot. It should classify messages into these intent groups:

1) Greeting: hello, hi, hey, good morning
2) Pricing: pricing, price, cost
3) Help: help
4) Contact: contact
5) About: about, what do you do, what does this bot do, who are you
6) Fallback: anything else

5) Reply composer
The reply composer returns short WhatsApp-friendly text. It should produce exactly the expected greeting response for “hello”: Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

6) Optional persistence layer
For this simple bot, persistence is optional. In-memory maps may be used for lightweight request deduplication or basic per-user counters. MongoDB may be used if message logs, audit trails, or user conversation history are needed.

7) Logging and observability
The service should log inbound request metadata such as messageId, from, projectId, platform, source, detected intent, and processing result. Logs should avoid storing sensitive full message content unless explicitly needed for debugging and allowed by policy.

Data model:
The bot can operate without a database. However, the following practical data model is recommended if MongoDB persistence is enabled.

1) MessageEvent
Purpose: stores inbound message metadata for debugging, analytics, and auditability.

Fields:
1) messageId: unique identifier from CookMyBots
2) from: sender identifier provided by CookMyBots
3) projectId: CookMyBots project identifier
4) platform: expected to be whatsapp
5) source: source identifier from CookMyBots
6) text: inbound user message, optionally redacted depending on privacy needs
7) normalizedText: trimmed lowercase version used for routing
8) intent: detected intent such as greeting, pricing, help, contact, about, or fallback
9) reply: response returned by the bot
10) timestamp: original event timestamp from CookMyBots
11) receivedAt: server receive time
12) processedAt: server processing completion time
13) status: processed, duplicate, rejected, or failed

2) UserState
Purpose: optional lightweight state for future personalization or test analytics.

Fields:
1) from: sender identifier
2) firstSeenAt: first message time
3) lastSeenAt: latest message time
4) messageCount: number of processed messages
5) lastIntent: most recent detected intent
6) lastReplyAt: latest reply time

3) IdempotencyRecord
Purpose: prevents duplicate processing if CookMyBots retries delivery of the same message.

Fields:
1) messageId: unique inbound message identifier
2) from: sender identifier
3) reply: reply that was generated for the original delivery
4) createdAt: time the message was first processed

For the initial version, an in-memory map keyed by messageId is sufficient if the service is expected to run as a single process and duplicate protection only needs to last for the current process lifetime. MongoDB is better if duplicate handling should survive restarts.

Key flows:
1) Greeting flow
A user sends “hello”, “hi”, “hey”, or “good morning” in WhatsApp. CookMyBots forwards the normalized message event to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp. The service verifies the webhook secret, detects the greeting intent, and returns: Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

2) Help flow
A user sends “help”. The bot returns a short list of available commands, for example: I can help with: help, pricing, contact, and about.

3) Pricing flow
A user sends “pricing”, “price”, or “cost”. The bot returns a simple pricing explanation, for example: Pricing is not configured yet. This test bot can show placeholder pricing information.

4) Contact flow
A user sends “contact”. The bot returns a placeholder support contact message, for example: You can contact support at support@example.com. Replace this with your real support contact.

5) About flow
A user sends “about” or asks what the bot does. The bot explains that it is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots, for example: I am WhatsApp Test Assistant, a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots.

6) Fallback flow
A user sends any other text. The bot responds naturally and confirms receipt, for example: Thanks, I received your message. Send help to see what I can do.

7) Unauthorized webhook flow
A request reaches POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp without the correct X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header. The service rejects the request and does not run bot logic.

8) Duplicate message flow
If messageId has already been processed, the service should return the same reply if available. This prevents inconsistent behavior during webhook retries.

9) Local test flow
A developer sends a sample request to POST /test with text such as “hello”. The service runs the same intent router and reply composer, then returns the reply. This verifies bot behavior without connecting to WhatsApp directly.

Tech stack:
The bot should use a single Node.js ESM service/process.

Allowed components:

1) Node.js ESM runtime for the bot brain service.
2) An HTTP server framework suitable for exposing POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp, GET /health, and POST /test.
3) Environment variables for configuration, especially CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET.
4) MongoDB only if persistence is needed for message logs, user state, or idempotency records.
5) Simple in-memory maps if persistence is optional or if only lightweight runtime state is needed.
6) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if future file or image handling is added.
7) Generic Node.js server in the cloud for deployment.

Strict transport boundary:

1) WhatsApp connectivity is handled by CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.
2) The bot must not call WhatsApp APIs directly.
3) The bot must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks.
4) The bot must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN, SESSIONID, or QRSESSION.
5) The bot must not include WhatsApp Web session setup, QR code login logic, or Meta webhook verification.

Required environment variables:

1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET: shared secret used to verify the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret request header.
2) NODEENV: runtime environment such as development or production.
3) PORT: HTTP server port.
4) MONGODBURI: optional, only if MongoDB persistence is enabled.

The service should remain simple and dependency-light because the first release is a deterministic command-style test bot.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Security
The production webhook must verify X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret using CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET. Unauthorized requests should be rejected before message processing. Logs should avoid exposing secrets and should avoid unnecessary sensitive message storage.

2) Reliability
The webhook should respond quickly and consistently. For this bot, all logic is local and deterministic, so replies should usually be generated immediately. Duplicate messageIds should be handled safely to support retry behavior.

3) Performance
The bot should be lightweight. String matching and response generation require minimal CPU and memory. A single Node.js process is sufficient for this test assistant.

4) Maintainability
Intent matching should be organized so future commands can be added without changing the transport layer. The webhook handler, validation, intent routing, reply composition, and persistence should remain separate responsibilities.

5) Observability
Each processed webhook should log request metadata, detected intent, response status, and processing duration. The /health endpoint should make basic uptime monitoring straightforward.

6) Privacy
Only store message content if needed. If MongoDB logging is enabled, keep retention limited and avoid storing unnecessary personal data.

7) User experience
Replies should be short, friendly, and professional. The bot should not send multiple messages for a single inbound message. It should not use markdown tables or long paragraphs.

8) Production readiness checklist
1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET configured before deployment.
2) /health returns a successful response.
3) /test confirms “hello” produces the expected greeting.
4) CookMyBots project points to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp.
5) No WhatsApp Cloud API, WhatsApp Web, QR session, or direct WhatsApp token configuration is present.
6) Logs confirm inbound messageId, intent, and successful reply generation.

Bot commands:
• help — Shows a short list of available bot commands. (usage: Send: help) [support]
• pricing — Returns a simple placeholder pricing explanation. The same intent should also match price and cost. (usage: Send: pricing) [sales]
• contact — Returns a placeholder support contact message that can later be replaced with real contact details. (usage: Send: contact) [support]
• about — Explains that this is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots. (usage: Send: about) [information]

DOCS.md.content:
# WhatsApp Test Assistant

WhatsApp Test Assistant is a simple CookMyBots managed WhatsApp bot brain.

CookMyBots handles the WhatsApp connection and transport. This service does not implement WhatsApp Cloud API setup, Meta webhook verification, WhatsApp Web sessions, QR login, phone number ID setup, or direct WhatsApp API calls.

## Endpoints

### GET /health

Returns a simple healthy JSON response for deployment checks.

Example response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "status": "healthy",
  "platform": "whatsapp"
}

### POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp

CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport forwards inbound WhatsApp messages to this endpoint.

Required header:

X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret: your configured CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Accepted JSON fields:

1) from
2) text
3) messageId
4) projectId
5) platform
6) source
7) timestamp

Successful response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "reply": "Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do."
}

Invalid or missing webhook secret returns an unauthorized JSON response.

### POST /test

Use this endpoint for local testing without WhatsApp.

Example body:

{
  "text": "hello"
}

Example response:

{
  "ok": true,
  "reply": "Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do."
}

## Supported messages

Send hello, hi, hey, or good morning to receive the welcome reply.

Send help to see available text commands.

Send pricing, price, or cost to receive placeholder pricing information.

Send contact to receive a placeholder support contact message.

Send about, what do you do, what does this bot do, or who are you to learn what the bot does.

Any other message receives a short acknowledgement.

## Public text commands

### help

Shows the available text commands.

Usage: send help

### pricing

Returns placeholder pricing information.

Usage: send pricing, price, or cost

### contact

Returns placeholder support contact information.

Usage: send contact

### about

Explains that this is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots.

Usage: send about

## Environment variables

### CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET

Required for the production webhook. CookMyBots sends this value in the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header.

### PORT

Optional server port. Defaults to 3000 when missing.

## Local setup

1) Install dependencies with npm install.
2) Copy .env.sample to .env.
3) Set CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET for webhook testing.
4) Start development mode with npm run dev.

## Local test examples

Start the server, then send a local test request to POST http://localhost:3000/test with this JSON body:

{
  "text": "hello"
}

To test the production-style webhook locally, send POST http://localhost:3000/webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp with the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header and a body like:

{
  "from": "local-user",
  "text": "help",
  "messageId": "test-message-1",
  "projectId": "local-project",
  "platform": "whatsapp",
  "source": "cookmybots",
  "timestamp": "2026-01-01T00:00:00.000Z"
}

## Deployment notes

Deploy this as one Node.js service. Configure PORT if your host requires it. Configure CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET in the CookMyBots deployment environment.

In CookMyBots, connect your WhatsApp number in the UI. CookMyBots will forward WhatsApp messages to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp and use the JSON reply returned by this service.

## Logging

The service logs startup, environment sanity booleans, webhook receipt, rejected secret verification, successful reply generation, and route failures.

Secrets are never logged.

README.md.content:
# WhatsApp Test Assistant

A WhatsApp-only bot brain for CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

CookMyBots handles WhatsApp connectivity. This repo only receives normalized message events, generates one short reply, and returns it as JSON.

## Endpoints

1) GET /health
Returns deployment health JSON.

2) POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp
Production CookMyBots inbound WhatsApp webhook. Requires X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret.

3) POST /test
Local testing endpoint. Requires only a JSON body with text.

## Environment

Copy .env.sample to .env and configure:

1) CMB_WHATSAPP_WEBHOOK_SECRET
2) PORT

No WhatsApp tokens, phone number IDs, QR sessions, or direct WhatsApp credentials are used.

## Run

1) npm install
2) npm run dev

## Test

POST http://localhost:3000/test

Body:

{
  "text": "hello"
}

Expected reply:

Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

See DOCS.md for full usage details.

- contact
- about

state.planMarkdown:
Bot: WhatsApp Test Assistant

Summary:
WhatsApp Test Assistant is a simple production-ready customer support test bot for WhatsApp. It runs as an independent Node.js ESM bot brain service and connects to WhatsApp only through CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

The bot does not manage WhatsApp sessions, QR codes, Meta webhook verification, phone number IDs, WhatsApp tokens, or direct WhatsApp API calls. CookMyBots handles all WhatsApp transport concerns. The service only receives normalized inbound message events from CookMyBots, decides what to reply, and returns the reply in the webhook response.

The first target behavior is intentionally simple: when a WhatsApp user sends “hello”, the bot should respond with: “Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.”

Product goals:
1) Provide a simple WhatsApp support test experience.
The bot should make it easy to confirm that a WhatsApp number connected in CookMyBots can send messages to the bot brain and receive replies.

2) Keep replies short and WhatsApp-friendly.
Responses should be concise, friendly, and professional. The bot should avoid long paragraphs, tables, or excessive follow-up messages.

3) Support a small command set.
The bot should understand greetings, help, pricing, contact, and about-style questions. It should also handle unknown messages gracefully by acknowledging that the message was received.

4) Stay transport-independent.
The bot project is only the brain service. It should not include WhatsApp Cloud API logic, WhatsApp Web session logic, QR session setup, phone number ID setup, or direct WhatsApp API calls.

5) Be production-ready despite simple behavior.
The service should include request validation, webhook secret verification, health checks, local testing support, structured logging, safe error handling, and clear environment configuration.

High-level architecture:
The system is a single Node.js ESM service running as a bot brain behind CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.

Runtime components:

1) HTTP server
The service exposes three main endpoints:

1) POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp
This is the production inbound webhook endpoint used by CookMyBots. It receives normalized WhatsApp message events, verifies the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header against CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET, routes the message to the bot logic, and returns JSON containing ok: true and reply.

2) GET /health
This endpoint confirms that the service is alive. It should return a simple health response suitable for uptime checks.

3) POST /test
This endpoint is for local development and QA. It accepts a test message payload similar to the CookMyBots webhook shape and returns the same reply decision without requiring live WhatsApp traffic. It should not send any WhatsApp messages directly.

2) Webhook security layer
The webhook endpoint must reject requests when the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header is missing or does not match CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET. This prevents unauthorized systems from triggering bot replies.

3) Request normalization layer
The webhook accepts these fields from CookMyBots: from, text, messageId, projectId, platform, source, and timestamp.

The bot should normalize text by trimming whitespace and comparing lowercase versions for command detection. The original text may be retained for logging or fallback replies.

4) Intent router
A simple deterministic router is enough for this test bot. It should classify messages into these intent groups:

1) Greeting: hello, hi, hey, good morning
2) Pricing: pricing, price, cost
3) Help: help
4) Contact: contact
5) About: about, what do you do, what does this bot do, who are you
6) Fallback: anything else

5) Reply composer
The reply composer returns short WhatsApp-friendly text. It should produce exactly the expected greeting response for “hello”: Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

6) Optional persistence layer
For this simple bot, persistence is optional. In-memory maps may be used for lightweight request deduplication or basic per-user counters. MongoDB may be used if message logs, audit trails, or user conversation history are needed.

7) Logging and observability
The service should log inbound request metadata such as messageId, from, projectId, platform, source, detected intent, and processing result. Logs should avoid storing sensitive full message content unless explicitly needed for debugging and allowed by policy.

Data model:
The bot can operate without a database. However, the following practical data model is recommended if MongoDB persistence is enabled.

1) MessageEvent
Purpose: stores inbound message metadata for debugging, analytics, and auditability.

Fields:
1) messageId: unique identifier from CookMyBots
2) from: sender identifier provided by CookMyBots
3) projectId: CookMyBots project identifier
4) platform: expected to be whatsapp
5) source: source identifier from CookMyBots
6) text: inbound user message, optionally redacted depending on privacy needs
7) normalizedText: trimmed lowercase version used for routing
8) intent: detected intent such as greeting, pricing, help, contact, about, or fallback
9) reply: response returned by the bot
10) timestamp: original event timestamp from CookMyBots
11) receivedAt: server receive time
12) processedAt: server processing completion time
13) status: processed, duplicate, rejected, or failed

2) UserState
Purpose: optional lightweight state for future personalization or test analytics.

Fields:
1) from: sender identifier
2) firstSeenAt: first message time
3) lastSeenAt: latest message time
4) messageCount: number of processed messages
5) lastIntent: most recent detected intent
6) lastReplyAt: latest reply time

3) IdempotencyRecord
Purpose: prevents duplicate processing if CookMyBots retries delivery of the same message.

Fields:
1) messageId: unique inbound message identifier
2) from: sender identifier
3) reply: reply that was generated for the original delivery
4) createdAt: time the message was first processed

For the initial version, an in-memory map keyed by messageId is sufficient if the service is expected to run as a single process and duplicate protection only needs to last for the current process lifetime. MongoDB is better if duplicate handling should survive restarts.

Key flows:
1) Greeting flow
A user sends “hello”, “hi”, “hey”, or “good morning” in WhatsApp. CookMyBots forwards the normalized message event to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp. The service verifies the webhook secret, detects the greeting intent, and returns: Hello, welcome to WhatsApp Test Assistant. Send help to see what I can do.

2) Help flow
A user sends “help”. The bot returns a short list of available commands, for example: I can help with: help, pricing, contact, and about.

3) Pricing flow
A user sends “pricing”, “price”, or “cost”. The bot returns a simple pricing explanation, for example: Pricing is not configured yet. This test bot can show placeholder pricing information.

4) Contact flow
A user sends “contact”. The bot returns a placeholder support contact message, for example: You can contact support at support@example.com. Replace this with your real support contact.

5) About flow
A user sends “about” or asks what the bot does. The bot explains that it is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots, for example: I am WhatsApp Test Assistant, a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots.

6) Fallback flow
A user sends any other text. The bot responds naturally and confirms receipt, for example: Thanks, I received your message. Send help to see what I can do.

7) Unauthorized webhook flow
A request reaches POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp without the correct X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret header. The service rejects the request and does not run bot logic.

8) Duplicate message flow
If messageId has already been processed, the service should return the same reply if available. This prevents inconsistent behavior during webhook retries.

9) Local test flow
A developer sends a sample request to POST /test with text such as “hello”. The service runs the same intent router and reply composer, then returns the reply. This verifies bot behavior without connecting to WhatsApp directly.

Tech stack:
The bot should use a single Node.js ESM service/process.

Allowed components:

1) Node.js ESM runtime for the bot brain service.
2) An HTTP server framework suitable for exposing POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp, GET /health, and POST /test.
3) Environment variables for configuration, especially CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET.
4) MongoDB only if persistence is needed for message logs, user state, or idempotency records.
5) Simple in-memory maps if persistence is optional or if only lightweight runtime state is needed.
6) Object storage or simple filesystem storage only if future file or image handling is added.
7) Generic Node.js server in the cloud for deployment.

Strict transport boundary:

1) WhatsApp connectivity is handled by CookMyBots managed WhatsApp transport.
2) The bot must not call WhatsApp APIs directly.
3) The bot must not implement WhatsApp Cloud API webhooks.
4) The bot must not require WHATSAPPTOKEN, WHATSAPPPHONENUMBERID, WHATSAPPVERIFYTOKEN, SESSIONID, or QRSESSION.
5) The bot must not include WhatsApp Web session setup, QR code login logic, or Meta webhook verification.

Required environment variables:

1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET: shared secret used to verify the X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret request header.
2) NODEENV: runtime environment such as development or production.
3) PORT: HTTP server port.
4) MONGODBURI: optional, only if MongoDB persistence is enabled.

The service should remain simple and dependency-light because the first release is a deterministic command-style test bot.

Non-functional requirements:
1) Security
The production webhook must verify X-CookMyBots-Webhook-Secret using CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET. Unauthorized requests should be rejected before message processing. Logs should avoid exposing secrets and should avoid unnecessary sensitive message storage.

2) Reliability
The webhook should respond quickly and consistently. For this bot, all logic is local and deterministic, so replies should usually be generated immediately. Duplicate messageIds should be handled safely to support retry behavior.

3) Performance
The bot should be lightweight. String matching and response generation require minimal CPU and memory. A single Node.js process is sufficient for this test assistant.

4) Maintainability
Intent matching should be organized so future commands can be added without changing the transport layer. The webhook handler, validation, intent routing, reply composition, and persistence should remain separate responsibilities.

5) Observability
Each processed webhook should log request metadata, detected intent, response status, and processing duration. The /health endpoint should make basic uptime monitoring straightforward.

6) Privacy
Only store message content if needed. If MongoDB logging is enabled, keep retention limited and avoid storing unnecessary personal data.

7) User experience
Replies should be short, friendly, and professional. The bot should not send multiple messages for a single inbound message. It should not use markdown tables or long paragraphs.

8) Production readiness checklist
1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET configured before deployment.
2) /health returns a successful response.
3) /test confirms “hello” produces the expected greeting.
4) CookMyBots project points to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp.
5) No WhatsApp Cloud API, WhatsApp Web, QR session, or direct WhatsApp token configuration is present.
6) Logs confirm inbound messageId, intent, and successful reply generation.

Bot commands:
• help — Shows a short list of available bot commands. (usage: Send: help) [support]
• pricing — Returns a simple placeholder pricing explanation. The same intent should also match price and cost. (usage: Send: pricing) [sales]
• contact — Returns a placeholder support contact message that can later be replaced with real contact details. (usage: Send: contact) [support]
• about — Explains that this is a test WhatsApp bot built with CookMyBots. (usage: Send: about) [information]

Intent matching should be organized so future commands can be added without changing the transport layer. The webhook handler, validation, intent routing, reply composition, and persistence should remain separate responsibilities.

5) Observability
Each processed webhook should log request metadata, detected intent, response status, and processing duration. The /health endpoint should make basic uptime monitoring straightforward.

6) Privacy
Only store message content if needed. If MongoDB logging is enabled, keep retention limited and avoid storing unnecessary personal data.

7) User experience
Replies should be short, friendly, and professional. The bot should not send multiple messages for a single inbound message. It should not use markdown tables or long paragraphs.

8) Production readiness checklist
1) CMBWHATSAPPWEBHOOKSECRET configured before deployment.
2) /health returns a successful response.
3) /test confirms “hello” produces the expected greeting.
4) CookMyBots project points to POST /webhook/cookmybots/whatsapp.
5) No WhatsApp Cloud API, WhatsApp Web, QR session, or direct WhatsApp token configuration is present.
6) Logs confirm inbound messageId, intent, and successful reply generation.
